title: Ronnies Party

jokes.text: Well, I hope we didn't catch the two of you with your pants down.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn show up unannounced with leftovers, implying they may have interrupted something.
jokes.punchline: Roland makes a suggestive innuendo about catching the Roses 'with their pants down.'
jokes.explanation: Fits Roland's crass but jovial character. The innuendo is fairly standard but the self-correction that follows elevates it slightly. Works because it establishes the scene's dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I hope we didn't catch you with your pants down.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Your pants are on.

jokes.text: I'm just joking, I can see they're on.
jokes.setup: Roland makes a pants-down innuendo.
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies he checked — which is somehow more mortifying than the original joke.
jokes.explanation: The self-correction is better than the original joke. Roland confirming he actually looked is a perfect character beat — oblivious to how that makes things worse. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: They literally eavesdropped to avoid catching them in compromising position

jokes.text: No, we actually listened outside to make sure.
jokes.setup: Roland claims he can see their pants are on.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn volunteers that they listened at the door before entering — making it even more invasive and funny.
jokes.explanation: This is the best beat in the three-part escalation. Jocelyn cheerfully revealing they eavesdropped to ensure privacy is delightfully intrusive and character-perfect — she means well but is completely oblivious to the violation.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, it's like a firm handshake, a good eye contact, maybe a little light touching on the arm. You gotta make 'em feel like they're the only person in the room.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's not really fun.

jokes.text: it was kind of a political schmoozefest. A lot of pressing of the flesh, and greasing palms, that kind of thing... It wasn't as much fun as it sounds.
jokes.setup: Roland explains why they didn't invite the Roses to their political gathering.
jokes.punchline: He says 'pressing the flesh and greasing palms... wasn't as much fun as it sounds' — delivered as if the physical imagery is genuinely appealing.
jokes.explanation: Roland's delivery of political clichés as though they sound fun is mild but character-consistent. The punchline is slightly weak because the setup does most of the work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how thoughtful. Leftovers. You know, in some cultures, regifting is considered an art form. In others, it's a cry for help. I'm starting to think yours might be both.

jokes.text: Oh well, who wants an invitation to big soiree when you can be met the very next morning with some table scraps.
jokes.setup: Roland explains the party wasn't meant as a slight — they just brought leftovers instead.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames the leftovers as 'table scraps' in a magnificently cutting, aristocratic manner.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Moira — effortlessly dignified outrage delivered as social commentary. The word choice ('soiree,' 'table scraps') is perfectly calibrated to her character and the contrast with the cheerful banality of Roland's gift is sharp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It means to wear scrubs.

jokes.text: Those two are not as simple minded as we thought. He is.
jokes.setup: Roland mentions people were talking about Moira (the other candidate) at the party, which Moira takes as a compliment.
jokes.punchline: After the Schitts leave, Moira upgrades her opinion of Jocelyn but maintains her assessment of Roland in one brutal, perfectly timed beat.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Two sentences, maximum information. The structural pivot — softening judgment only to redirect it — is perfectly executed. 'He is' as a complete sentence is devastating and characterizes Moira with perfect economy. High quotability, strong rewatch value for timing appreciation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking... Roland and Jocelyn have been quite helpful to us.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, no. Let's not get carried away. Roland is exactly as simple-minded as we thought.

jokes.text: David, Ted said that I had to wear scrubs, but what does that mean really? / Um, I think it means you have to wear scrubs.
jokes.setup: Alexis is starting her first job at a vet clinic and is confused about the dress code.
jokes.punchline: David delivers the most literal possible answer to an obviously rhetorical question.
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling dynamic — Alexis seeking validation, David refusing to give it. His literalism undercuts her completely. Works because both characters are perfectly in-voice.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing those scrubs. They're so unflattering and they come in, like, hospital blue? I need them in a color that doesn't make me look like I'm actively dying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, they're medical uniforms, not a capsule wardrobe.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly! Which is why this is a feminist issue. We're forcing women to wear these shapeless, soul-crushing garments that strip away their individuality and femininity. It's basically a form of oppression.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're a receptionist.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A receptionist with rights, David.

jokes.text: Whatever happened to empowering women's sexuality? / I don't think sex appeal is the guiding principle behind nursing uniforms.
jokes.setup: Alexis complains about having to wear unflattering scrubs.
jokes.punchline: David points out that medical uniforms are not, in fact, designed with sex appeal in mind.
jokes.explanation: Solid Alexis character work — her worldview where everything should be sexy clashes with the mundane reality David points out. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Sex appeal isn't the point of nursing uniforms.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, have you ever seen a sexy nurse costume?

jokes.text: Obviously you've never been out for Halloween, David.
jokes.setup: David says sex appeal isn't the guiding principle behind nursing uniforms.
jokes.punchline: Alexis implies that sexy nurse costumes on Halloween prove nurses are inherently sexualizable — a complete non sequitur that perfectly reflects her worldview.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's logic is internally consistent with her character and completely absurd. The snap delivery and implied condescension toward David is a strong character beat. Her Halloween reference as evidence is delightfully flawed reasoning.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This isn't my first job, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh really? And what was your previous job?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was a taste tester for edible nail polish.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not a job, Alexis. That's just you eating nail polish.

jokes.text: Putting your name on a line of edible nail polish isn't what I would call having a job.
jokes.setup: Alexis insists she's had jobs before.
jokes.punchline: David reveals the 'job' — lending her name to edible nail polish — which is both absurd and perfectly Alexis.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'edible nail polish' is what makes this land. It's absurd enough to feel completely plausible for Alexis's pre-Schitt's Creek life. Strong character integration — this couldn't belong to anyone else.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was very hands-on with creating the flavors. I tested every single one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's actually something we need to talk about. The product line had a poisoning incident.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? From my flavors?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say your hands-on approach may have been a bit too hands-on.

jokes.text: I was very hands on, David. I came up with all the flavours by myself.
jokes.setup: David dismisses her nail polish venture as not a real job.
jokes.punchline: Alexis defends her 'hands-on' involvement — which amounts to taste-testing flavors.
jokes.explanation: The 'hands on' phrasing for licking nail polish is a nice touch of unintentional wordplay. Alexis's genuine pride in this contribution deepens the character joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: People are getting poisoned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's not like we're using lead paint or anything. It's all imported from overseas.

jokes.text: Even the one that poisoned all those people?
jokes.setup: Alexis boasts about coming up with all the flavors for her edible nail polish line.
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals one of the flavors caused a poisoning — treated as a minor footnote.
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The casual escalation to 'poisoned all those people' is perfectly dark, perfectly David. The breezy delivery of mass poisoning as a conversational aside is exactly the kind of dark absurdism Schitt's Creek does best. 'All those people' is the precise word choice that pushes it from good to great — implying scale without being specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like a flamingo that got into a car accident.

jokes.text: David, the factory in Guangzhou assured us that it was lead-free.
jokes.setup: David calls out the poisoning caused by her nail polish.
jokes.punchline: Alexis responds not with remorse but with a corporate defense — the factory said it was fine.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Guangzhou' and 'lead-free' elevates this from a simple deflection to a rich character moment. Alexis isn't defensive — she's genuinely citing a quality assurance claim as absolution. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I mean, why are you worried about him? He's rich.

jokes.text: You look like a stick of gum.
jokes.setup: Alexis emerges in her scrubs, presumably in a bright color (likely green or blue).
jokes.punchline: David's assessment is both cutting and absurdly specific.
jokes.explanation: Economy is almost perfect — three words that perfectly capture the visual, David's snark, and his fashion-consciousness. 'Stick of gum' is an unexpected and specific enough image to earn the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These taxidermy peacocks are a legitimate business expense.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: How are multiple dead birds a business expense?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're for the store's ambiance. It's called branding.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You have five of them.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, one peacock says 'rustic.' Five peacocks say 'I have a problem.'

jokes.text: She's an IT manager, Gerald. She makes more money than both of us combined.
jokes.setup: Johnny is apparently worried about how much someone is withdrawing or spending; we hear Stevie on the phone in the background.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's overheard phone call reveals she's managing a complex personal situation involving financial dynamics — establishing Stevie's dry competence.
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing background detail that humanizes Stevie but doesn't land as a full joke. Works as texture rather than a punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not arriving too early, that's desperate. And I'm not arriving too late, that's rude. I'm arriving at the exact moment that says, 'I have a life, but I'm also very responsible.'

jokes.text: I don't think that expense card is long for this world.
jokes.setup: Johnny is uncomfortable spending on what seem like unnecessary luxuries (peacocks) while his financial situation is uncertain.
jokes.punchline: He delivers a quietly resigned assessment of the expense card's lifespan.
jokes.explanation: Characterizes Johnny's practical anxiety against Moira's extravagance. 'Not long for this world' is a nicely understated way to express financial dread. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Where are the rest of your scrubs?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm wearing scrubs.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You're only wearing the top.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I thought the top and bottom were optional alternatives. Like, you could wear one or the other.

jokes.text: I have four more taxidermy peacocks coming this afternoon. / Oh, I can pay cash for those. That's fine.
jokes.setup: Johnny is already worried about the expense card.
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals she's ordered FOUR taxidermy peacocks — and immediately offers cash as if that resolves the absurdity of the purchase.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of taxidermy peacocks (plural, four of them) is wonderfully absurd. Moira's solution — cash — sidesteps the obvious question of WHY she needs four dead peacocks. Strong character integration; this is peak Moira extravagance.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You really shouldn't expose your legs like that. It's dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh Ted, are you saying my legs are dangerous?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, I'm saying that you're exposing yourself to the sun without protection, and you're at risk for skin cancer, premature aging, and sun poisoning.

jokes.text: Hey! Sorry I'm a bit late. Um, I wasn't sure what the etiquette was for this kind of thing, so I walked around the block a little bit. / Oh, you can show up early, or on time. Early is probably even better.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives late to her first day at Ted's vet clinic.
jokes.punchline: Ted patiently explains that 'early or on time' is actually the correct etiquette for a job — a completely normal thing that Alexis apparently didn't know.
jokes.explanation: Ted's patience in explaining basic workplace etiquette to Alexis is a sweet character beat. The 'walked around the block' detail is a nice Alexis touch — she overthought showing up for a job she apparently had no idea how to approach.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to be such a good secretary. I'm honestly going to be better than the last one. I'm going to file things. I'm going to file them so hard.

jokes.text: Those bare legs are dangerous. / Ted! / No, I'm serious. The animals carry bugs or ticks that can burrow into your bare skin.
jokes.setup: Alexis has worn only half her scrubs (apparently just the top), showing bare legs.
jokes.punchline: Ted's concern about 'bare legs' sounds like flirting but is entirely professional — he's worried about tick-borne illness.
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection — the setup implies a flirtatious comment and the punchline is medical genuinely. Ted's professionalism vs. Alexis's expectation of being perceived as attractive is a great dynamic. Works on multiple levels.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, darling. That jacket is absolutely stunning. Is that a custom Armani?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It's from the Walmart.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's very... functional. The pleating alone suggests someone understands the importance of structure. Though I would have perhaps suggested a more refined fabric. Cashmere? Silk-blend? Anything really that whispers rather than shouts.

jokes.text: I'll just dive right into this. You won't even miss your last secretary once I get finished with these puppies.
jokes.setup: Alexis is assigned filing duties at the vet clinic.
jokes.punchline: 'Puppies' is an unintentional double meaning — she means the files, but saying it in a vet's office is funny. Also her overconfidence about filing is immediately undercut.
jokes.explanation: The 'puppies' reference in a vet clinic is mildly amusing wordplay. Her breezy overconfidence about basic office tasks is good character work. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So what was Jocelyn's party like?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It was fine. She had some cheese. It wasn't good cheese.

jokes.text: I found you a loaner pair [of scrub pants]. Helen must have left them. [Cut to Alexis in comically ill-fitting pants]
jokes.setup: Ted finds Alexis a spare pair of scrub bottoms since she didn't bring her own.
jokes.punchline: The loaner pants are presumably enormously oversized/ill-fitting, defeating Alexis's attempt to be stylish — confirmed by her sarcastic 'just perfect.'
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of Alexis in ill-fitting borrowed scrubs is inferred from her 'just perfect' sarcasm. Strong physical comedy that undercuts her vanity. The rewatch value would be higher if we could see the pants.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think what you've done here is very... dairy-forward. Which is interesting, because I find that when one hosts, one typically considers the lactose-intolerant contingent. But what do I know? I'm just someone who's thrown dinner parties for heads of state.

jokes.text: New khakis I see. / Are you enjoying your coffee? / I was.
jokes.setup: Moira launches into political small talk, complimenting Ronnie's khakis.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's 'I was' implies Moira's presence has ruined the enjoyment of the coffee — a perfectly dry, minimal response.
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's 'I was' is a masterclass in economy. Two words that communicate volumes: she was fine until Moira showed up, she's not pleased, and she'll be giving nothing away. Perfect character integration for both Moira's oblivious political schmoozing and Ronnie's no-nonsense terseness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, could you put your weight behind me?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: [reaction]

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, I meant— your physical strength. Not your... mass.

jokes.text: If anyone knows how to put a lactose intolerant in the hospital, it's our Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Moira asks how Jocelyn's party was and Ronnie says 'good, lots of dairy.'
jokes.punchline: Moira cheerfully implies Jocelyn's food hospitality is so dairy-heavy it constitutes a medical hazard.
jokes.explanation: Lovely dark aside. Moira delivers this with theatrical warmth as if it's a compliment to Jocelyn — 'our Jocelyn' is particularly good, implying affectionate familiarity while being subtly savage. Specific and well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It's not a date.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, of course not. I just meant... it's a thing. A meeting thing. That we're both attending.

jokes.text: I'm hoping I might persuade you to put your weight behind me. [beat] Political weight. I now realize 'clout' was the word I was looking for.
jokes.setup: Moira is asking Ronnie for her political endorsement.
jokes.punchline: Moira tells Ronnie she wants her 'weight' behind her — immediately realizing 'weight' could be interpreted as a comment on Ronnie's body — and self-corrects hilariously.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The setup is Moira at her most imperious, then the slow-dawning realization that she's said something that could be taken as a size comment — and her correction to 'clout' is pitch-perfect. The structure (statement, pause, clarification, retrospective solution) is textbook cringe comedy. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, it's like when you really love someone, you just have to find a way to make it work, no matter what obstacles are in your way. Like, my friend's cousin dated this guy who was basically royalty in another country, and there were all these international laws and political issues keeping them apart, but they just... they figured it out. Well, actually, no, they didn't. He had to marry someone else for like, a trade agreement or something. But the point is, they tried!

jokes.text: It's a date. / No. No, it's not. I wouldn't call it that. / No, I didn't... I didn't mean... / Uh! Just... / No, I...
jokes.setup: Moira accepts Ronnie's invitation to meet her friends with 'It's a date.'
jokes.punchline: Ronnie immediately shuts it down — not a date — and both parties fumble through the subsequent awkwardness.
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire mutual embarrassment as they both try to clarify is well-structured cringe. Ronnie's flat, definitive 'No. No, it's not.' is the funniest beat. Seeds the later misunderstanding about the demographic.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: How's the filing coming along?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, so good. I've been really getting into it. I organized all the files by color, and then by texture, and then I realized that I was just going through a really difficult time emotionally, so I've been using work as a way to avoid dealing with my feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: ...That's great, Alexis.

jokes.text: I once dated this Sultan's nephew who was forbidden to talk to me or even to look at me, and we made it work for like half a regime change, so...
jokes.setup: Alexis is advising Doris about navigating a complicated romantic situation (her boyfriend is allergic to her cat).
jokes.punchline: Alexis's romantic advice comes from experience dating a Sultan's nephew — a detail so casually absurd it perfectly characterizes her jet-set pre-Schitt's Creek life.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Alexis character joke. 'Half a regime change' is the standout phrase — it treats a political revolution as a unit of time for measuring a relationship, which is both brilliantly absurd and deeply specific to who Alexis is. Delivered casually, as if this is normal romantic context. High quotability, strong rewatch value. One of the episode's best.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I appreciate your concern, but Doris has been bringing Mr. Whiskers in here every week for the past six months convinced he has a different terminal illness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, maybe he does!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Last week it was feline leukemia. The week before, a brain tumor. Two weeks ago, she was certain he'd been poisoned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: He's fine, Alexis. He's a very healthy cat. But his owner is... a lot.

jokes.text: So how's the filing coming? / I have been swamped. Doris's cat is like totally high maintenance.
jokes.setup: Ted checks in on Alexis's filing progress.
jokes.punchline: Alexis has abandoned the files to counsel Doris about her cat situation — and genuinely believes this counts as being 'swamped.'
jokes.explanation: Alexis's inability to distinguish between emotional support and actual work is a great character beat. 'Swamped' by a client's cat problems is perfectly Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm here if you need anything.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, I need you to pick up my prescription.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I meant more like... emotionally.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Great. Pick up my prescription emotionally then.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was trying to give you space to—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, pick up my prescription.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, okay.

jokes.text: Okay, well, what am I supposed to do? Poor thing is like desperate for a man.
jokes.setup: Ted points out that Doris comes in every week worried about her cat.
jokes.punchline: Alexis diagnoses Doris's real problem as being desperate for a man — which, to be fair, is accurate — and treats this as her professional responsibility.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's social diagnosis is correct but completely misapplied to her job function. Mild but characterful.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't babysit. I have plans.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Great. You're babysitting tomorrow at seven.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you hear what I just said?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I did. Babysitting. Tomorrow. Seven.

jokes.text: Um... I don't know, someone hung up?
jokes.setup: Ted asks why there are three calls on hold.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's best theory is that 'someone hung up' — a completely non-explanatory explanation delivered as if it's helpful.
jokes.explanation: The uncertainty attached to a simple fact (she put three calls on hold, one apparently disconnected) reveals her complete detachment from the job. 'Someone hung up?' rising inflection is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: Wow... Well, if there's anything I can do, just... / I need someone to take care of my stepdaughter. / Okay. Well, if there's anything I can do, just... / I need you to take care of my stepdaughter.
jokes.setup: David offers vague platitudes of support to Stevie who is clearly in crisis.
jokes.punchline: The exact same exchange repeats twice — David offers the same empty offer, Stevie repeats the same specific request, David continues to dodge.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly structured. The repetition of the exact same exchange — David giving the non-offer, Stevie repeating her need — is both very funny and character-perfect. David's social cowardice is exposed; Stevie's no-nonsense persistence is endearing. The structural decision to repeat verbatim rather than vary shows strong comedic writing confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: My cat died. What do I do with the body?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I... well, there are pet cremation services, or you could have a burial in your yard, or—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Why does everyone keep dying?

jokes.text: 'Kay, I don't think that's a very good idea. / I'm gonna drop her off at four.
jokes.setup: David finally explicitly says babysitting isn't a good idea.
jokes.punchline: Stevie ignores his objection entirely and announces the drop-off time — the matter is settled.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the running exchange. Stevie doesn't argue, doesn't negotiate — she just announces the logistics. David's protest is treated as irrelevant. Their dynamic is captured perfectly in two lines.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is... we're trapped in a box with broken dreams and false hope?

jokes.text: I brought you some trail mix, but I spilled it in my purse.
jokes.setup: Doris arrives at the vet clinic, presumably to check on Mittens.
jokes.punchline: She announces she brought trail mix but immediately explains she's ruined it — the gesture and its failure delivered in one breath as if equally noteworthy.
jokes.explanation: Sweet and slightly pathetic — Doris's failed kindness gesture is a nice character touch. The combination of good intention and immediate failure in one sentence is charming.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm babysitting tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, whose child did you agree to this?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, don't answer that. I need to get my phone ready to document this.

jokes.text: Hey, I was thinking about what you said yesterday and, um, I think... / Now is not a good time.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to bring up something personal with Ted while Mittens is apparently dead on the table.
jokes.punchline: Ted's flat shutdown — 'Now is not a good time' — without explanation, while clearly handling a dead cat, is perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: Works because we don't yet know why 'now' isn't a good time — the reveal comes shortly after. The deadpan shutdown functions both as a joke and as tension-building.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, when you're at the motel, can you check the lost and found for my anxiety medication?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, that's not how anxiety medication works.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm anxious that I lost it, so technically it is.

jokes.text: How are things with Mittens? / He's dead. / Ew!
jokes.setup: Alexis, trying to make conversation after being shut down, asks about Mittens.
jokes.punchline: Ted's blunt 'He's dead' — and Alexis's completely tone-deaf 'Ew!' response — is a perfect comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: Ted's flat delivery of 'He's dead' is perfect — no softening, no context, just the fact. But Alexis's 'Ew!' is what makes this exceptional. Instead of sympathy or professionalism, she goes straight to disgust. Character-perfect for Alexis, and the economy is flawless. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, the good news is that Mr. Whiskers is no longer suffering from feline diabetes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And he's finally at a weight where he could fit through a standard doorway.

jokes.text: Sounds like something you'd find at a serial killer's house.
jokes.setup: The motel clerk offers Alexis a Rummoli box filled with puzzle pieces from a shelf.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's casual comparison of the motel's entertainment offerings to serial killer décor.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Rummoli box filled with puzzle pieces' as an unsettling image is nicely observed. 'Serial killer's house' is a fun leap that works because she's not wrong — it IS an uncanny combination.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so Fluffy is like, totally in a better place now. Like, she's been released from the physical constraints of her body, and she's like, spiritually transcended or whatever. She's probably like, really happy right now, you know? Like, she's experiencing ultimate freedom.

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: She was hit by a car.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly! So like, one minute she's stuck in this earthly realm, and the next minute she's just like, whoooosh, gone. It's actually really beautiful when you think about it.

jokes.text: You're joking. / Mm-mm. I wish I was joking. / But you hate kids. / Mm-hmm. That's correct. / Oh my God. Can I watch? / No. / Or video tape? / No, you... no. / Can I bring friends? / No.
jokes.setup: Alexis reveals she'll be babysitting tonight.
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire escalation of requests — watch, video tape, bring friends — and Alexis's increasingly exasperated 'no's' is a great rhythm joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalating request structure (watch → videotape → bring friends) is well-built and the rhythm of the exchange is tight. 'But you hate kids / That's correct' is particularly good — Alexis owns it without shame.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You need to be forceful with women, Johnny. They respect a man who takes charge. Now, I once knew a landscaper named—actually, never mind, that's not relevant.

jokes.text: Um, 'cause I'm a fully capable human being. Um, if you happen to find any Xanax lying around anywhere, can you just... let me know about that?
jokes.setup: The clerk asks if she's sure she can handle babysitting on her own.
jokes.punchline: Alexis asserts her capability while immediately undermining it by asking about available Xanax.
jokes.explanation: The self-contradicting structure is perfectly executed — assertion of competence immediately followed by Xanax request. The request itself is funny (she's asking a motel for controlled substances casually), and Alexis's character is richly captured. 'Lying around anywhere' is a great detail.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, I've done extensive research on cosmetic procedures. I've practically memorized every before and after photo on the internet. I could tell you the difference between a chemical peel and a microdermabrasion in my sleep. Not that I would ever need either one, obviously.

jokes.text: ...these treats are actually gonna go on sale next week, so maybe you just wanna hold off until then? / Well, I think he'd really like them. / Yeah... I'm sure he would really like them... if he wasn't... dead.
jokes.setup: Doris reaches for cat treats while Alexis knows Mittens is dead.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's elaborate stall — inventing a fictional upcoming sale — collapses into the blunt, poorly-delivered revelation that Mittens is dead.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's fumbling attempt at delicacy — the fake sale as a stall — paired with the eventual blurt-out is excellently structured cringe. The trailing '...if he wasn't... dead' with evident discomfort is a great performance beat that works even in transcript form. Strong character integration: Alexis trying to be considerate but completely failing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's a bug on the bed! There's a bug on the bed! Oh my God, there's a bug on the bed!

jokes.text: What? / Yeah, he's dead. / But! / But the good news is, you're not.
jokes.setup: Doris is shocked that Mittens is dead.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's attempt at consolation — 'the good news is, you're not' — is the most absurdly minimal silver lining possible.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'The good news is, you're not' as consolation for a dead pet is brilliantly minimal and completely Alexis — she's trying, she's just catastrophically bad at this. The bar for the silver lining (being alive) is set so low it loops back to being funny. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was at a dinner party last week, and we ended up playing charades. David Sedaris was there, and let me tell you, that man cannot act. He was trying to do 'Breakfast at Tiffany's' and nobody could guess it. I finally said, 'David, you're a professional writer—surely you can convey a simple concept through physical movement.' He just looked at me and said, 'I'm better with words.'

jokes.text: But you're free, Doris, and, in a way, so is Mittens.
jokes.setup: Doris is grieving Mittens.
jokes.punchline: Alexis frames the cat's death as liberation — for Doris AND for Mittens — treating mortality as an emancipatory narrative.
jokes.explanation: Alexis applying spiritual/motivational framing to a dead cat is a great character beat. 'In a way, so is Mittens' is the dark-funny pivot. Her improvised grief counseling continues to be wonderfully misguided.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, it's not a lesbian gathering. It's just a group of women who are interested in the same things.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, so it's a book club?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, it's not a book club. It's just... a gathering.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, then what do they have in common?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They all have male husbands.

jokes.text: Ew! No, we don't have to talk about that right now, okay?
jokes.setup: Doris asks practical questions about what to do with Mittens' body (box, bury?).
jokes.punchline: Alexis, who is supposed to be providing comfort, responds to 'bury him?' with 'Ew!' — exactly the same response she gave to the cat being dead.
jokes.explanation: The callback to her earlier 'Ew!' reinforces her character consistency — she has the same reaction to death and to the logistics of dealing with death. Good running character bit.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, what did I tell you about talking to the mayor's wife?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was just making conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You asked her about her bunions, John. Her bunions.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: She brought them up!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And now you're going to stand in the corner and think about what you've done.

jokes.text: The upside... Oh! is that Albert from down the hall can now come over for dinner without worrying about his allergies threatening his life. / Hey. I think it's time that we find you a new love of your life.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to find an upside to Mittens dying.
jokes.punchline: The upside she lands on — the boyfriend can now visit — immediately pivots to replacing Mittens entirely as the 'love of her life.'
jokes.explanation: Alexis's accidental brilliance — by trying to be comforting she actually solves Doris's problem. 'New love of your life' is a slightly tone-deaf but ultimately correct reframe. Good character arc in miniature.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is the key demographic?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Women, 18 to 49.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you meant a key demographic. Like someone who has a key.

jokes.text: John, wait. I didn't tell you earlier because I didn't want you to back out on me, but you're going to be the only man here this evening.
jokes.setup: Moira and John are about to arrive at Ronnie's party; Moira has been withholding information
jokes.punchline: The withholding is that John will be the only man — Moira knew this would alarm him but needed him there
jokes.explanation: Setup joke for what's coming — functions more as plot planting than a strong joke on its own. The comedy is in the anticipation.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You want to know who your core demographic is? It's people who have nothing better to do than watch you fail.

jokes.text: Moira, I have a feeling not every woman here is a lesbian. / Oh, John, you love to think women are flirting with you.
jokes.setup: Johnny pulls Moira aside at the party to suggest the group may not be what they assumed.
jokes.punchline: Moira completely misreads his concern — interpreting his observation about the women's orientation as his ego about being flirted with.
jokes.explanation: Moira's deflection reveals how convinced she is that all women are in love with John — and how invested she is in the evening going well. The misread is characterful and the line is sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So how many vacation days do I get?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Two weeks a year.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, and then how many more after that?

jokes.text: I feel really good about this, and I think you just approach these women like you did Paula, our landscaper. Very strong, very forceful.
jokes.setup: Moira coaches John on how to interact with the women at Ronnie's party
jokes.punchline: Her advice — be 'very strong, very forceful' with women based on how John handled the landscaper — is bad advice delivered with complete confidence; also suggests John's 'strength' with Paula was bossy rather than charming
jokes.explanation: Sets up the coming disaster — Moira's confident bad advice. The Paula the landscaper detail is specific enough to be funny but doesn't land as a standalone punchline.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, puberty is a natural part of growing up. Your body is going to change, you're going to have new feelings, and it's all perfectly normal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The important thing is to take care of yourself. Invest in a good skincare routine, obviously. And Egyptian cotton sheets—at least 1000 thread count. Trust me, when you're going through all these hormonal changes, you want to be sleeping on something that feels like a cloud.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Seriously, I cannot stress this enough. Polyester blends are going to make everything worse. You need to find a luxury linen supplier immediately.

jokes.text: Has that actress had work done too? Um, yeah, you can always tell because their upper lip sort of... I mean, like, puffs a bit.
jokes.setup: John is at Ronnie's party, the only man, watching TV with the group
jokes.punchline: John casually and confidently holds forth on plastic surgery tells — a subject he has apparently developed expertise in, possibly from being married to Moira
jokes.explanation: Mild character beat — Johnny fitting in by discussing plastic surgery suggests he's absorbed more of Moira's world than he realizes. Functional but not sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm a business owner. I came out as a business owner.

jokes.text: No, I was just talking to someone over there who has a husband. A male husband.
jokes.setup: Moira accuses John of thinking the women are flirting with him.
jokes.punchline: Johnny specifies 'a male husband' — the qualifier being both unnecessary and revealing of how lost he is.
jokes.explanation: The redundancy of 'male husband' is perfectly absurd — he's so flustered he's over-specifying to make his point. Economy is near-perfect. Johnny's fumbling attempt to clarify without saying 'lesbian' is character comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've had many jobs over the years. I was a spokesperson for a medical device company, a hostess, a real estate agent, and I once prioritized money over children.

jokes.text: Entrepreneurs. Everyone here is a member of the Women's Business Association.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what 'key demographic' Ronnie meant — clearly expecting the answer to be 'lesbian.'
jokes.punchline: Ronnie reveals it's the Women's Business Association — the Roses' assumption was entirely wrong.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the entire evening's misunderstanding. The audience and the Roses discover simultaneously that they've been catastrophically wrong. Ronnie's matter-of-fact delivery makes it better — she had no idea they'd misread the situation this badly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was at the grocery store the other day, and I ran into one of my constituents. Such a lovely woman. We chatted for a bit, and I mentioned my recent bout with hemorrhoids.

jokes.text: All I'm missing is my teleprompter!
jokes.setup: Moira is about to address the businesswomen — she's been preparing for a very different audience.
jokes.punchline: Her theatrical confidence in the face of a completely unexpected audience reveals how much she's winging it — and how she'd prefer mechanical support.
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical save — pivoting from panic to performance — is characterful. The teleprompter joke signals both her background and her current disorientation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just assumed, you know, that it was a group of... well, I thought it was going to be a more... how do I put this delicately... a more estrogen-based organization.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Not that there's anything wrong with that! I just... I pictured it as being more of a, you know, a feminine gathering.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What I'm trying to say is, I made an assumption based on the name, and clearly I was mistaken, which is embarrassing.

jokes.text: Business Association. / Yes, I heard her.
jokes.setup: Johnny repeats 'Business Association' in disbelief.
jokes.punchline: Moira, already pivoting to performance mode, snaps that she heard — she doesn't need his panic mirrored back at her.
jokes.explanation: The snap of 'Yes, I heard her' is a great character beat — Moira shutting down John's panic so she can focus. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I started out working in construction, doing manual labor. Then I moved into heavy equipment operation. Now I'm running this real estate business.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: And you people came here with nothing but designer luggage and a country club membership that doesn't work anymore.

jokes.text: I had a six-and-a-half season career as an actress in television, which I put on hold to devote all my time and energy to raise two million dollars for charity. / And two children. / Oh, yes, and two children.
jokes.setup: A woman asks about balancing work and raising a family — Moira is asked directly.
jokes.punchline: Moira rattles off her career and charity work before Johnny has to remind her about the children — which she then lists as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional joke. Moira listing 'two million dollars for charity' BEFORE her children — and only adding them when prompted — is devastating character comedy. 'Oh, yes, and two children' as a correction is perfect. The structure (achievement, bigger achievement, prompt, afterthought) is textbook. This moment encapsulates everything about Moira in one exchange. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not prejudiced. I have a very diverse group of friends.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have a Black friend, an Asian friend, a gay friend...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, actually, my gay friend is also Black, so that's two birds with one stone.

jokes.text: It's not everyone who can tell an old woman that her cat is dead, and set up a love connection, and sell our most expensive marble urn in the same three-minute conversation.
jokes.setup: Ted is recapping what Alexis accomplished with Doris.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Alexis also sold Doris their most expensive marble urn during the grief counseling — apparently upselling a funeral product — is delightfully absurd.
jokes.explanation: The callback revelation that Alexis unconsciously upsold a marble urn during a sympathy conversation is a great structural payoff. Ted's genuine admiration is the perfect tone — he's impressed rather than appalled. Strong economy in the list structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I'm very progressive. I have several lesbian friends. Well, I don't know them personally, but I've seen documentaries.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And I understand that in lesbian relationships, one of them is typically the man. Which I find fascinating.

jokes.text: Oh! No, I think it was about, um, vacation days. Yeah, you had mentioned two weeks. So I guess I was just wondering, um, how many two-week vacations are available per year?
jokes.setup: Ted asks if Alexis had something she wanted to say to him (implying something personal/romantic).
jokes.punchline: The big reveal is that Alexis's burning question was about vacation day logistics — specifically whether 'two weeks' meant one two-week vacation or multiple.
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection — the setup implies Alexis has something emotionally meaningful to say to Ted; the punchline is that she's negotiating PTO on her first day. The specificity of 'how many two-week vacations per year' is wonderfully entitled. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look, I know I've been hard on this town, but I want you to know that I appreciate what you've all done for us. And I'm going to try to be more positive going forward. So, moving ahead, I'm going to make a real effort to embrace the good things in life. You know, like a good meal. A delicious meal. Because there's nothing quite like sitting down to a really exquisite plate of food and just... savoring it. Mmm. Yes. And I think that's something that a man can really appreciate.

jokes.text: Um, how many two-week vacations are available per year? Well, tell you what, why don't we start with one and then we'll just go from there.
jokes.setup: Alexis claims she was trying to ask Ted something earlier — vacation days
jokes.punchline: She asks how many TWO-WEEK vacations she gets per year (not vacation weeks — two-week vacations, plural) as her first-day employment question
jokes.explanation: The plural framing — 'two-week vacations' as a recurring unit — perfectly captures Alexis's background and expectations. Ted's patient 'let's start with one' suggests he's already learned to calibrate for her. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Honestly? Nothing is more natural... um, aside from maybe the thread count in these sheets, but that's okay! 'Cause you're a woman now!
jokes.setup: Alexis is apparently babysitting a girl who has just gotten her first period, and is attempting to be comforting.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's consolation drifts into a complaint about thread count before snapping back to the 'woman now' pep talk — revealing her complete lack of preparation for this.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The thread count digression in the middle of a 'you're a woman now' speech is brilliantly Alexis — her mind wanders to luxury linens even during genuine emotional support. Strong character integration; the pivot back to 'you're a woman now!' is perfectly earnest.

jokes.text: Um, and nothing about that is embarrassing. / I'm not embarrassed, I'm scared!
jokes.setup: Alexis is reassuring the girl getting her first period.
jokes.punchline: The girl's correction — 'I'm not embarrassed, I'm scared!' — reveals that Alexis has been responding to the wrong emotional register entirely.
jokes.explanation: Alexis has been comforting for the wrong thing. The child's correction reveals how badly Alexis has misread the room — she addressed embarrassment when fear was the actual issue. Well-structured: Alexis's approach reveals as much about her as about the situation.

jokes.text: I think my sister has some wonderful products in there for you. Uh, health accessories, so feel free to help yourself to those.
jokes.setup: Alexis needs to provide sanitary products to the girl but doesn't have them — she refers to David's store.
jokes.punchline: 'Health accessories' is Alexis's delicate euphemism for period products — which is somehow both more and less embarrassing than just saying it.
jokes.explanation: 'Health accessories' as a label for period products is a nicely Alexis-specific euphemism — she's both trying to be delicate and clearly uncomfortable with specificity. Character-consistent.

jokes.text: Equality. Freedom. Marriage. / These are the rights of every community, and even more specifically... the business community.
jokes.setup: Moira begins her speech, which was clearly prepared for a different audience.
jokes.punchline: Her grand list of rights — Equality, Freedom, Marriage — pivots to 'the business community,' revealing she's tried to shoehorn her other speech into a new context.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the desperate pivot. 'Equality, Freedom, Marriage' clearly belonged to a different speech and Moira's '...and even more specifically, the business community' is the scramble to land it somewhere coherent. Reveals her improvising under pressure brilliantly.

jokes.text: One of my top priorities will be to change people's misguided and ignorant perceptions of women like you. / You did not choose this life! / No, each one of you was born to be an entrepreneur.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to adapt her speech to businesswomen but is still using the framing from her previous (wrong) speech.
jokes.punchline: 'You did not choose this life!' delivered to businesswomen — as if entrepreneurship is an involuntary condition — is a wonderful misfire that she somehow recovers from.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. 'You did not choose this life!' as a rallying cry for people who literally chose to start businesses is the perfect collision of Moira's prepared speech and wrong context. The recovery — 'born to be an entrepreneur' — is equally funny because it doesn't actually fix the problem. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: One of my top priorities will be to... to change people's misguided and ignorant perceptions of women like you. You did not choose this life! No, each one of you was born to be an entrepreneur.
jokes.setup: Moira continues the speech she was going to give to LGBT women, now applied to businesswomen
jokes.punchline: 'You did not choose this life! No, each one of you was born to be an entrepreneur.' — What was written as a passionate LGBT rights statement becomes absurd applied to small business owners
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak. 'You did not choose this life! No, each one of you was born to be an entrepreneur' is genuinely brilliant — the rhetorical structure of coming-out advocacy applied to accounting and gravel businesses is absurd and perfectly executed. High originality, perfect character integration. Moira's theatrical commitment to an increasingly wrong speech is Schitt's Creek at its best.

jokes.text: You run your own business? / Household staff, yes.
jokes.setup: The businesswomen ask Moira if she ran her own business.
jokes.punchline: Moira's answer — 'Household staff, yes' — reframes managing domestic employees as entrepreneurship.
jokes.explanation: Moira's complete sincerity in claiming household staff management as business experience is perfectly calibrated. She's not lying — she genuinely sees it this way. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: and you, Tara, you're very concerned about your inverted nipples. Which I assumed was public domain; there were a lot of you around when she told me.
jokes.setup: Moira is demonstrating she's learned about each businesswoman to show she pays attention.
jokes.punchline: She reveals Tara's extremely private medical concern in front of the whole group — and defends herself by noting it was mentioned in a crowd.
jokes.explanation: The episode's standout joke. The specific choice of 'inverted nipples' as the overshared detail is perfectly unexpected — specific enough to be shocking, intimate enough to be mortifying. Then 'which I assumed was public domain; there were a lot of you around when she told me' is a masterpiece of Moira's self-serving logic. She defends the violation by noting the original disclosure had witnesses. The character integration is flawless — only Moira would do this, and only Moira would have this defense. Extremely quotable. Compares favorably to the vasectomy example anchor.

jokes.text: I had my first job at the age of ten, sweeping up hair in a beauty salon. I will never forget the value of a hard day's work.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to connect with the working women by emphasizing her work ethic.
jokes.punchline: The humble origin story — sweeping hair at ten — sits hilariously incongruent with everything we know about Moira's lifestyle and values.
jokes.explanation: The gap between Moira's 'humble beginnings' claim and her actual personality is the joke. 'I will never forget the value of a hard day's work' — delivered by someone who ordered four taxidermy peacocks this morning — is a good irony beat.

jokes.text: Yeah, I think your wife's gonna do okay. / Well, she pulled it off, didn't she? / Considering she came in, well, we both did... thinking everyone was... / Black? / No. I mean that everyone was leaning... / Younger?
jokes.setup: Johnny and Ronnie debrief after the party. Johnny tries to reference their initial assumption.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie offers 'Black?' as an interpretation of what they thought — John scrambles to correct, Ronnie offers 'Younger?' — both guesses are wrong and the hole keeps getting deeper.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe sequence. Ronnie's 'Black?' is perfectly placed — a genuinely uncomfortable guess that forces Johnny to scramble. 'Younger?' as the second guess prolongs the discomfort. The rhythm of Johnny's unfinished sentences and Ronnie's guesses is expertly structured. Shows great confidence in the writing — letting the gap be filled wrongly twice.

jokes.text: John, we should go. / No, what I mean is that, uh, that I am very comfortable seeing women with other women.
jokes.setup: Johnny is making things worse trying to explain what he meant.
jokes.punchline: Instead of stopping, Johnny produces the most awkward possible alternative phrasing.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's attempt to recover produces an equally problematic statement. The escalation of self-imposed damage is a classic comedy structure. Moira's 'we should go' as damage control is well-timed.

jokes.text: Moira and I have been very, very supportive of the LGBTQ community. Oh, John... John. For... Did I leave out a letter?
jokes.setup: John is continuing to dig out of his hole; Moira tries to stop him
jokes.punchline: John overcorrects into an earnest LGBTQ declaration, then asks if he 'left out a letter' — suggesting he's tracking the acronym like a spelling exercise rather than from genuine familiarity
jokes.explanation: 'Did I leave out a letter?' is a beautifully specific punchline — John is so anxious about political correctness that he reduces LGBTQ to a spelling task. Moira's 'Oh, John... John' perfectly sells the exhaustion of being married to him. This lands as one of the stronger jokes in the closing sequence.

jokes.text: Moira and I have been very, very supportive of the LGBTQ community. / Oh, John... John. / For... / Did I leave out a letter?
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to establish his credentials as an ally, which just continues the hole-digging.
jokes.punchline: 'Did I leave out a letter?' — Johnny's genuine concern that his LGBTQ acronym was incomplete is the perfect final beat of this sequence.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional button on the entire sequence. 'Did I leave out a letter?' is a perfect Johnny Rose joke — his main concern in this disaster is whether his LGBTQ acronym was complete. It reveals his well-meaning cluelessness perfectly. The moment pivots from cringe to warmth because his panic about inclusivity is genuine. Extremely quotable and character-perfect. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Thank you so much, Ronnie. I love your friends and I really loved that dip. Oh, the dip was incredible, really, for men and women.
jokes.setup: The Roses are wrapping up and saying their goodbyes to Ronnie
jokes.punchline: John's addendum that the dip was 'incredible, really, for men and women' — an inclusive declaration about dip that is simultaneously his continued awkward effort to signal open-mindedness and completely inane
jokes.explanation: Perfect button for the episode. 'Incredible, really, for men and women' is the funniest possible exit line — John's clumsy allyship has now extended to food. The specificity of the qualifier ('for men and women') is exactly as absurd as it should be. Strong closing joke.

jokes.text: I love your friends and I really loved that dip. / Oh, the dip was incredible, really, for men and women.
jokes.setup: The evening is wrapping up and Moira is making a gracious exit.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's addendum — 'the dip was incredible, really, for men and women' — is a final fumbled attempt to signal his non-prejudice using a dip.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final joke is excellent. 'The dip was incredible, really, for men and women' — Johnny's last-ditch attempt to establish his inclusivity through the medium of appetizers — is a perfect button on the entire party storyline. Absurd, character-perfect, and immediately quotable.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira meet with a key group of people, Alexis struggles with her new job, David offer's emotional support to his boss' daughter.
insight: Ronnie's abrasive charm carries a solid mid-season pivot with 53 jokes at steady 2.45-per-minute pace.
